WHERE i.quantity_on_hand>0
  AND p.sku NOT IN (
      SELECT DISTINCT soi.sku FROM sales_order_items soi
      JOIN sales_orders so ON soi.so_id=so.so_id
      WHERE so.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
  )
GROUP BY p.category
ORDER BY provision_required DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Tiered pricing analysis — supplier unit price brackets across purchase volume bands
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name, poi.sku, p.product_name,
       CASE WHEN poi.ordered_quantity<100 THEN '<100 units'
            WHEN poi.ordered_quantity<500 THEN '100-499 units'
            WHEN poi.ordered_quantity<1000 THEN '500-999 units'
            ELSE '1000+ units' END AS volume_band,
       AVG(poi.unit_price) AS avg_price,
       COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.supplier_name, poi.sku, p.product_name,
  CASE WHEN poi.ordered_quantity<100 THEN '<100 units'
       WHEN poi.ordered_quantity<500 THEN '100-499 units'
       WHEN poi.ordered_quantity<1000 THEN '500-999 units'
       ELSE '1000+ units' END
ORDER BY s.supplier_name, poi.sku, volume_band;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Does order volume affect the unit price we pay to each supplier?
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name, poi.sku, p.product_name,
       CASE WHEN poi.ordered_quantity<100 THEN 'small'
            WHEN poi.ordered_quantity<500 THEN 'medium'
            ELSE 'large' END AS order_size,
       ROUND(AVG(poi.unit_price),2) AS avg_unit_price
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.supplier_name, poi.sku, p.product_name,
  CASE WHEN poi.ordered_quantity<100 THEN 'small' WHEN poi.ordered_quantity<500 THEN 'medium' ELSE 'large' END
ORDER BY s.supplier_name, poi.sku, avg_unit_price;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Volume-tiered pricing — how does average unit cost change across small medium and large order sizes?
output:
SELECT poi.sku, p.product_name,
       SUM(CASE WHEN poi.ordered_quantity<100 THEN poi.ordered_quantity ELSE 0 END) AS small_vol,
       ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN poi.ordered_quantity<100 THEN poi.unit_price END),2) AS small_avg_price,
       SUM(CASE WHEN poi.ordered_quantity BETWEEN 100 AND 499 THEN poi.ordered_quantity ELSE 0 END) AS med_vol,
       ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN poi.ordered_quantity BETWEEN 100 AND 499 THEN poi.unit_price END),2) AS med_avg_price,
       SUM(CASE WHEN poi.ordered_quantity>=500 THEN poi.ordered_quantity ELSE 0 END) AS large_vol,
       ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN poi.ordered_quantity>=500 THEN poi.unit_price END),2) AS large_avg_price
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY poi.sku, p.product_name
HAVING COUNT(*)>=5
ORDER BY poi.sku;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Stratified vendor base — suppliers grouped by annual spend into strategic preferred and tactical tiers
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name, s.category,
       SUM(poi.line_total) AS annual_spend,
       CASE WHEN SUM(poi.line_total)>=5000000 THEN 'STRATEGIC'
            WHEN SUM(poi.line_total)>=1000000 THEN 'PREFERRED'
            WHEN SUM(poi.line_total)>=100000  THEN 'APPROVED'
            ELSE 'TACTICAL' END AS vendor_tier
FROM suppliers s
JOIN purchase_orders po ON s.supplier_id=po.supplier_id
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON po.po_id=poi.po_id
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND YEAR(po.order_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.supplier_name, s.category
ORDER BY annual_spend DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: How are our suppliers stratified by total spend — which tier does each vendor fall into?
output:
WITH spend AS (
    SELECT po.supplier_id, SUM(poi.line_total) AS total
    FROM purchase_orders po JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON po.po_id=poi.po_id
    WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND YEAR(po.order_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())
    GROUP BY po.supplier_id
)
SELECT s.supplier_name,
       sp.total AS annual_spend,
       NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY sp.total DESC) AS spend_quartile,
       CASE NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY sp.total DESC)
           WHEN 1 THEN 'STRATEGIC' WHEN 2 THEN 'PREFERRED'
           WHEN 3 THEN 'APPROVED' ELSE 'TACTICAL' END AS stratified_tier
FROM spend sp
JOIN suppliers s ON sp.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
ORDER BY annual_spend DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Supplier spend quartile breakdown — stratified ranking from highest to lowest
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name, s.category,
       SUM(poi.line_total) AS spend,
       NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY SUM(poi.line_total) DESC) AS quartile
FROM suppliers s
JOIN purchase_orders po ON s.supplier_id=po.supplier_id
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON po.po_id=poi.po_id
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.supplier_name, s.category
ORDER BY spend DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Demand overshoot — months where actual demand exceeded forecast by more than 25%
output:
SELECT df.sku, p.product_name, df.forecast_period,
       df.forecasted_quantity, df.actual_quantity,
       ROUND(100.0*(df.actual_quantity-df.forecasted_quantity)/NULLIF(df.forecasted_quantity,0),1) AS overshoot_pct
FROM demand_forecasts df
JOIN products p ON df.sku=p.sku
WHERE df.actual_quantity IS NOT NULL
  AND df.actual_quantity > df.forecasted_quantity*1.25
ORDER BY overshoot_pct DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Which SKUs had actual demand overshoot the forecast by over 25% last year?
output:
SELECT df.sku, p.product_name, df.forecast_period,
       df.forecasted_quantity, df.actual_quantity,
       ROUND(100.0*(df.actual_quantity-df.forecasted_quantity)/NULLIF(df.forecasted_quantity,0),1) AS excess_pct
FROM demand_forecasts df
JOIN products p ON df.sku=p.sku
WHERE df.actual_quantity > df.forecasted_quantity*1.25
  AND df.forecast_period >= FORMAT(DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE()),'yyyy-MM')
ORDER BY excess_pct DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: How often does actual demand overshoot the forecast and by how much on average?
output:
SELECT df.sku, p.product_name,
       COUNT(*) AS periods_overshooting,
       ROUND(AVG(100.0*(df.actual_quantity-df.forecasted_quantity)/NULLIF(df.forecasted_quantity,0)),1) AS avg_overshoot_pct
FROM demand_forecasts df
JOIN products p ON df.sku=p.sku
WHERE df.actual_quantity > df.forecasted_quantity
  AND df.actual_quantity IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY df.sku, p.product_name
HAVING COUNT(*)>=2
ORDER BY avg_overshoot_pct DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Backordering exposure — open customer orders where stock is insufficient to fulfil
output:
SELECT soi.sku, p.product_name,
       SUM(soi.ordered_quantity-COALESCE(soi.fulfilled_quantity,0)) AS backorder_qty,
       COALESCE(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand),0) AS available_stock,
       GREATEST(0, SUM(soi.ordered_quantity-COALESCE(soi.fulfilled_quantity,0))
                   - COALESCE(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand),0)) AS unfillable_qty
FROM sales_order_items soi
JOIN sales_orders so ON soi.so_id=so.so_id
JOIN products p ON soi.sku=p.sku
LEFT JOIN inventory i ON soi.sku=i.sku
WHERE so.status IN ('OPEN','CONFIRMED')
GROUP BY soi.sku, p.product_name
HAVING GREATEST(0,SUM(soi.ordered_quantity-COALESCE(soi.fulfilled_quantity,0))-COALESCE(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand),0))>0
ORDER BY unfillable_qty DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Which open orders cannot be fulfilled from current stock — backordering risk?
output:
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name,
       COALESCE(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand),0) AS stock,
       SUM(soi.ordered_quantity-COALESCE(soi.fulfilled_quantity,0)) AS open_demand,
       GREATEST(0,SUM(soi.ordered_quantity-COALESCE(soi.fulfilled_quantity,0))-COALESCE(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand),0)) AS backorder_risk
FROM products p
LEFT JOIN inventory i ON p.sku=i.sku
JOIN sales_order_items soi ON p.sku=soi.sku
JOIN sales_orders so ON soi.so_id=so.so_id
WHERE so.status IN ('OPEN','CONFIRMED')
GROUP BY p.sku, p.product_name
HAVING GREATEST(0,SUM(soi.ordered_quantity-COALESCE(soi.fulfilled_quantity,0))-COALESCE(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand),0))>0
ORDER BY backorder_risk DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Total backordering liability by product category — quantity and value unable to ship
output:
SELECT p.category,
       SUM(GREATEST(0,soi.ordered_quantity-COALESCE(soi.fulfilled_quantity,0)-COALESCE(i.quantity_on_hand,0))) AS backorder_units,
       SUM(GREATEST(0,soi.ordered_quantity-COALESCE(soi.fulfilled_quantity,0)-COALESCE(i.quantity_on_hand,0))*soi.unit_price) AS backorder_value
FROM sales_order_items soi
JOIN sales_orders so ON soi.so_id=so.so_id
JOIN products p ON soi.sku=p.sku
LEFT JOIN inventory i ON soi.sku=i.sku
WHERE so.status IN ('OPEN','CONFIRMED')
GROUP BY p.category
ORDER BY backorder_value DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Postponement candidates — products that can have final assembly deferred closer to demand
output:
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name, p.category,
       COUNT(b.child_sku) AS bom_components,
       SUM(i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost) AS finished_goods_stock,
       COALESCE(SUM(comp_inv.component_stock)*p.unit_cost,0) AS component_stock_value
FROM products p
JOIN bom b ON p.sku=b.parent_sku
LEFT JOIN inventory i ON p.sku=i.sku
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT b2.parent_sku, SUM(i2.quantity_on_hand) AS component_stock
    FROM bom b2 JOIN inventory i2 ON b2.child_sku=i2.sku
    GROUP BY b2.parent_sku
) comp_inv ON p.sku=comp_inv.parent_sku
WHERE p.product_type='FINISHED_GOODS'
GROUP BY p.sku, p.product_name, p.category, p.unit_cost, comp_inv.component_stock
HAVING COUNT(b.child_sku)>=3
ORDER BY finished_goods_stock DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Which finished goods have enough components in stock to support postponed assembly?
output:
SELECT b.parent_sku, p.product_name,
       COUNT(b.child_sku) AS component_count,
       SUM(CASE WHEN i.quantity_on_hand >= b.quantity_required THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS components_available,
       COUNT(b.child_sku) - SUM(CASE WHEN i.quantity_on_hand >= b.quantity_required THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS components_short
FROM bom b
JOIN products p ON b.parent_sku=p.sku
LEFT JOIN inventory i ON b.child_sku=i.sku
GROUP BY b.parent_sku, p.product_name
ORDER BY components_available DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: BOM component availability check to support postponement planning for production runs
output:
SELECT wo.wo_number, p_fg.product_name AS product,
       b.child_sku, p_comp.product_name AS component,
       b.quantity_required*wo.planned_qty AS required,
       COALESCE(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand),0) AS available,
       IIF(COALESCE(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand),0)>=b.quantity_required*wo.planned_qty,'READY','SHORT') AS status
FROM work_orders wo
JOIN bom b ON wo.product_sku=b.parent_sku
JOIN products p_fg ON wo.product_sku=p_fg.sku
JOIN products p_comp ON b.child_sku=p_comp.sku
LEFT JOIN inventory i ON b.child_sku=i.sku
WHERE wo.status='PLANNED'
GROUP BY wo.wo_number, p_fg.product_name, b.child_sku, p_comp.product_name, b.quantity_required, wo.planned_qty
ORDER BY status, wo.wo_number;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Opportunistic buying window — SKUs where current price is below 12-month average
output:
WITH avg_price AS (
    SELECT poi.sku, AVG(poi.unit_price) AS avg_12m
    FROM purchase_order_items poi
    JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
    WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
    GROUP BY poi.sku
),
latest_price AS (
    SELECT poi.sku, poi.unit_price AS latest
    FROM purchase_order_items poi
    JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
    WHERE po.order_date=(
        SELECT MAX(po2.order_date) FROM purchase_orders po2
        JOIN purchase_order_items poi2 ON po2.po_id=poi2.po_id
        WHERE poi2.sku=poi.sku AND po2.status!='CANCELLED'
    )
)
SELECT ap.sku, p.product_name,
       lp.latest AS current_price,
       ROUND(ap.avg_12m,2) AS avg_12m_price,
       ROUND(100.0*(ap.avg_12m-lp.latest)/NULLIF(ap.avg_12m,0),1) AS discount_vs_avg_pct
FROM avg_price ap
JOIN latest_price lp ON ap.sku=lp.sku
JOIN products p ON ap.sku=p.sku
WHERE lp.latest < ap.avg_12m*0.90
ORDER BY discount_vs_avg_pct DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Are there any SKUs where the latest purchase price is more than 10% below the annual average — a potential opportunistic buy?
output:
SELECT poi.sku, p.product_name,
       MIN(poi.unit_price) AS lowest_recent,
       AVG(poi.unit_price) AS avg_price,
       ROUND(100.0*(AVG(poi.unit_price)-MIN(poi.unit_price))/NULLIF(AVG(poi.unit_price),0),1) AS discount_pct
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY poi.sku, p.product_name
HAVING MIN(poi.unit_price) < AVG(poi.unit_price)*0.90
ORDER BY discount_pct DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Identify opportunistic procurement windows — SKUs whose current price dips below historical average
output:
SELECT sp.sku, p.product_name, s.supplier_name,
       sp.unit_price AS current_rate,
       hist.avg_historical,
       ROUND(100.0*(hist.avg_historical-sp.unit_price)/NULLIF(hist.avg_historical,0),1) AS saving_pct
FROM sourcing_products sp
JOIN products p ON sp.sku=p.sku
JOIN suppliers s ON sp.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
JOIN (
    SELECT poi.sku, AVG(poi.unit_price) AS avg_historical
    FROM purchase_order_items poi
    JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
    WHERE po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-2,GETDATE()) AND po.status!='CANCELLED'
    GROUP BY poi.sku
) hist ON sp.sku=hist.sku
WHERE sp.status='APPROVED' AND sp.unit_price < hist.avg_historical*0.92
ORDER BY saving_pct DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Proactive stock replenishment — products where days cover will drop below lead time within 7 days
output:
WITH daily AS (
    SELECT sku, SUM(quantity)/30.0 AS rate
    FROM inventory_transactions
    WHERE transaction_type='OUTBOUND' AND transaction_date>=DATEADD(DAY,-30,GETDATE())
    GROUP BY sku
)
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name, p.lead_time_days,
       SUM(i.quantity_on_hand) AS stock,
       ROUND(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand)/NULLIF(d.rate,0),1) AS current_days_cover,
       ROUND((SUM(i.quantity_on_hand)-d.rate*7)/NULLIF(d.rate,0),1) AS days_cover_in_7_days
FROM products p
JOIN inventory i ON p.sku=i.sku
JOIN daily d ON p.sku=d.sku
GROUP BY p.sku, p.product_name, p.lead_time_days, d.rate
HAVING ROUND((SUM(i.quantity_on_hand)-d.rate*7)/NULLIF(d.rate,0),1) < p.lead_time_days
ORDER BY days_cover_in_7_days;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Which SKUs need proactive reorder action before stock falls below the lead time cover?
output:
WITH daily_rate AS (
    SELECT sku, SUM(quantity)/30.0 AS daily
    FROM inventory_transactions WHERE transaction_type='OUTBOUND'
      AND transaction_date>=DATEADD(DAY,-30,GETDATE())
    GROUP BY sku
)
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name, p.lead_time_days,
       SUM(i.quantity_on_hand) AS stock,
       ROUND(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand)/NULLIF(dr.daily,0),1) AS days_cover
FROM products p
JOIN inventory i ON p.sku=i.sku
JOIN daily_rate dr ON p.sku=dr.sku
GROUP BY p.sku, p.product_name, p.lead_time_days, dr.daily
HAVING ROUND(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand)/NULLIF(dr.daily,0),1) <= p.lead_time_days*1.25
ORDER BY days_cover;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Proactive demand signal — items where sales velocity is accelerating month over month
output:
WITH monthly AS (
    SELECT soi.sku,
           FORMAT(so.order_date,'yyyy-MM') AS month,
           SUM(soi.ordered_quantity) AS qty
    FROM sales_order_items soi JOIN sales_orders so ON soi.so_id=so.so_id
    WHERE so.order_date>=DATEADD(MONTH,-3,GETDATE()) AND so.status!='CANCELLED'
    GROUP BY soi.sku, FORMAT(so.order_date,'yyyy-MM')
)
SELECT m.sku, p.product_name,
       MAX(CASE WHEN m.month=FORMAT(DATEADD(MONTH,-2,GETDATE()),'yyyy-MM') THEN m.qty END) AS two_months_ago,
       MAX(CASE WHEN m.month=FORMAT(DATEADD(MONTH,-1,GETDATE()),'yyyy-MM') THEN m.qty END) AS last_month,
       MAX(CASE WHEN m.month=FORMAT(GETDATE(),'yyyy-MM') THEN m.qty END) AS this_month
FROM monthly m
JOIN products p ON m.sku=p.sku
GROUP BY m.sku, p.product_name
HAVING MAX(CASE WHEN m.month=FORMAT(GETDATE(),'yyyy-MM') THEN m.qty END)
       > MAX(CASE WHEN m.month=FORMAT(DATEADD(MONTH,-1,GETDATE()),'yyyy-MM') THEN m.qty END)
   AND MAX(CASE WHEN m.month=FORMAT(DATEADD(MONTH,-1,GETDATE()),'yyyy-MM') THEN m.qty END)
       > MAX(CASE WHEN m.month=FORMAT(DATEADD(MONTH,-2,GETDATE()),'yyyy-MM') THEN m.qty END)
ORDER BY this_month DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Reactive procurement — purchase orders raised as emergency responses to stockout events
output:
SELECT po.po_number, s.supplier_name, poi.sku, p.product_name,
       po.order_date AS emergency_po_date,
       se.start_date AS stockout_started,
       DATEDIFF(DAY,se.start_date,po.order_date) AS days_after_stockout,
       po.total_amount
FROM purchase_orders po
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON po.po_id=poi.po_id
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
JOIN stockout_events se ON poi.sku=se.sku
  AND po.order_date BETWEEN se.start_date AND DATEADD(DAY,14,se.start_date)
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED'
ORDER BY days_after_stockout;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Were any purchase orders placed reactively within 14 days of a stockout event starting?
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name, poi.sku, p.product_name,
       se.start_date AS stockout_date,
       po.order_date AS reactive_po_date,
       DATEDIFF(DAY,se.start_date,po.order_date) AS reaction_lag_days
FROM stockout_events se
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON se.sku=poi.sku
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
WHERE po.order_date BETWEEN se.start_date AND DATEADD(DAY,14,se.start_date)
  AND po.status!='CANCELLED'
  AND se.start_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
ORDER BY reaction_lag_days;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Reactive versus proactive procurement split — what percentage of POs are emergency driven?
output:
WITH reactive AS (
    SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT po.po_id) AS reactive_pos
    FROM purchase_orders po
    JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON po.po_id=poi.po_id
    JOIN stockout_events se ON poi.sku=se.sku
      AND po.order_date BETWEEN se.start_date AND DATEADD(DAY,14,se.start_date)
    WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_pos,
       r.reactive_pos,
       COUNT(*)-r.reactive_pos AS proactive_pos,
       ROUND(100.0*r.reactive_pos/NULLIF(COUNT(*),0),1) AS reactive_pct
FROM purchase_orders po
CROSS JOIN reactive r
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY r.reactive_pos;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Synchronised replenishment schedule — purchase orders aligned to production start dates within 3 days
output:
SELECT wo.wo_number, p_fg.product_name AS finished_good,
       b.child_sku, p_comp.product_name AS component,
       po.po_number, po.expected_delivery_date,
       wo.start_date AS production_start,
       ABS(DATEDIFF(DAY,po.expected_delivery_date,wo.start_date)) AS alignment_days
FROM work_orders wo
JOIN bom b ON wo.product_sku=b.parent_sku
JOIN products p_fg ON wo.product_sku=p_fg.sku
JOIN products p_comp ON b.child_sku=p_comp.sku
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON b.child_sku=poi.sku
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
WHERE wo.status='PLANNED'
  AND po.status IN ('APPROVED','SHIPPED')
  AND ABS(DATEDIFF(DAY,po.expected_delivery_date,wo.start_date))<=3
ORDER BY wo.start_date;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Has the component arrival been synchronised with the production schedule for all planned work orders?
output:
SELECT wo.wo_number, p_fg.product_name,
       wo.start_date,
       COUNT(b.child_sku) AS components_needed,
       SUM(CASE WHEN po.expected_delivery_date <= wo.start_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS components_synced,
       SUM(CASE WHEN po.expected_delivery_date > wo.start_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS components_late_arrival
FROM work_orders wo
JOIN bom b ON wo.product_sku=b.parent_sku
JOIN products p_fg ON wo.product_sku=p_fg.sku
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON b.child_sku=poi.sku
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
WHERE wo.status='PLANNED' AND po.status IN ('APPROVED','SHIPPED')
GROUP BY wo.wo_number, p_fg.product_name, wo.start_date
ORDER BY components_late_arrival DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Component delivery synchronisation status for upcoming production runs
output:
SELECT wo.wo_number, p.product_name AS product,
       wo.start_date,
       SUM(CASE WHEN po.expected_delivery_date<=wo.start_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS on_time_components,
       COUNT(b.child_sku) AS total_components,
       IIF(SUM(CASE WHEN po.expected_delivery_date>wo.start_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)>0,'AT RISK','SYNCHRONISED') AS delivery_sync_status
FROM work_orders wo
JOIN products p ON wo.product_sku=p.sku
JOIN bom b ON wo.product_sku=b.parent_sku
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON b.child_sku=poi.sku
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
WHERE wo.status='PLANNED' AND po.status IN ('APPROVED','SHIPPED')
GROUP BY wo.wo_number, p.product_name, wo.start_date
ORDER BY delivery_sync_status DESC, wo.start_date;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Bucketed inventory ageing — stock grouped into 30-day age brackets by last outbound date
output:
WITH last_out AS (
    SELECT sku, MAX(transaction_date) AS last_movement
    FROM inventory_transactions WHERE transaction_type='OUTBOUND'
    GROUP BY sku
)
SELECT p.category,
       SUM(CASE WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,lo.last_movement,GETDATE())<=30  THEN i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost ELSE 0 END) AS aged_0_30,
       SUM(CASE WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,lo.last_movement,GETDATE()) BETWEEN 31 AND 90  THEN i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost ELSE 0 END) AS aged_31_90,
       SUM(CASE WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,lo.last_movement,GETDATE()) BETWEEN 91 AND 180 THEN i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost ELSE 0 END) AS aged_91_180,
       SUM(CASE WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,lo.last_movement,GETDATE())>180 THEN i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost ELSE 0 END) AS aged_over_180
FROM inventory i
JOIN products p ON i.sku=p.sku
LEFT JOIN last_out lo ON i.sku=lo.sku
GROUP BY p.category
ORDER BY aged_over_180 DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: How does our inventory value break down into 30 60 90 and 180-day age buckets?
output:
WITH last_move AS (
    SELECT sku, MAX(transaction_date) AS last_out
    FROM inventory_transactions WHERE transaction_type='OUTBOUND'
    GROUP BY sku
)
SELECT
    SUM(CASE WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,lm.last_out,GETDATE())<=30  THEN i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost ELSE 0 END) AS bucket_0_30,
    SUM(CASE WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,lm.last_out,GETDATE()) BETWEEN 31 AND 60  THEN i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost ELSE 0 END) AS bucket_31_60,
    SUM(CASE WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,lm.last_out,GETDATE()) BETWEEN 61 AND 90  THEN i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost ELSE 0 END) AS bucket_61_90,
    SUM(CASE WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,lm.last_out,GETDATE()) BETWEEN 91 AND 180 THEN i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost ELSE 0 END) AS bucket_91_180,
    SUM(CASE WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,lm.last_out,GETDATE())>180 OR lm.last_out IS NULL THEN i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost ELSE 0 END) AS bucket_over_180
FROM inventory i
JOIN products p ON i.sku=p.sku
LEFT JOIN last_move lm ON i.sku=lm.sku
WHERE i.quantity_on_hand>0;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Inventory ageing bucketed by last movement — how much stock is stale beyond 90 days?
output:
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name,
       DATEDIFF(DAY,MAX(it.transaction_date),GETDATE()) AS days_since_movement,
       SUM(i.quantity_on_hand) AS qty,
       SUM(i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost) AS value,
       CASE WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,MAX(it.transaction_date),GETDATE())<=30 THEN '0-30'
            WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,MAX(it.transaction_date),GETDATE())<=90 THEN '31-90'
            WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,MAX(it.transaction_date),GETDATE())<=180 THEN '91-180'
            ELSE '180+' END AS age_bucket
FROM inventory i
JOIN products p ON i.sku=p.sku
LEFT JOIN inventory_transactions it ON i.sku=it.sku AND it.transaction_type='OUTBOUND'
WHERE i.quantity_on_hand>0
GROUP BY p.sku, p.product_name, p.unit_cost
HAVING DATEDIFF(DAY,MAX(it.transaction_date),GETDATE())>90 OR MAX(it.transaction_date) IS NULL
ORDER BY value DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Prioritise open purchase requisitions by value and urgency for today's approval queue
output:
SELECT pr.requisition_number, e.employee_name, e.department,
       pr.priority, pr.total_estimated_value,
       DATEDIFF(DAY,pr.created_date,GETDATE()) AS days_waiting,
       ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           ORDER BY CASE pr.priority WHEN 'CRITICAL' THEN 1 WHEN 'HIGH' THEN 2 ELSE 3 END,
                    pr.total_estimated_value DESC
       ) AS approval_queue_position
FROM purchase_requisitions pr
JOIN employees e ON pr.created_by=e.employee_id
WHERE pr.status='PENDING_APPROVAL'
ORDER BY approval_queue_position;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: How should I prioritise today's approval queue — which requisitions are most urgent?
output:
SELECT pr.requisition_number, pr.priority,
       pr.total_estimated_value, e.department,
       DATEDIFF(DAY,pr.created_date,GETDATE()) AS days_pending
FROM purchase_requisitions pr
JOIN employees e ON pr.created_by=e.employee_id
WHERE pr.status='PENDING_APPROVAL'
ORDER BY CASE pr.priority WHEN 'CRITICAL' THEN 1 WHEN 'HIGH' THEN 2 WHEN 'MEDIUM' THEN 3 ELSE 4 END,
         days_pending DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Ranked approval backlog — requisitions sorted to prioritise critical and high-value items
output:
SELECT pr.requisition_number, pr.priority,
       pr.total_estimated_value,
       DATEDIFF(DAY,pr.created_date,GETDATE()) AS age_days,
       RANK() OVER (
           ORDER BY CASE pr.priority WHEN 'CRITICAL' THEN 1 WHEN 'HIGH' THEN 2 ELSE 3 END,
                    pr.total_estimated_value DESC,
                    pr.created_date ASC
       ) AS priority_rank
FROM purchase_requisitions pr
WHERE pr.status='PENDING_APPROVAL'
ORDER BY priority_rank;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Monetise slow-moving stock — estimated revenue from liquidating inventory over 180 days old
output:
WITH last_out AS (
    SELECT sku, MAX(transaction_date) AS last_movement
    FROM inventory_transactions WHERE transaction_type='OUTBOUND' GROUP BY sku
)
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name, p.category,
       SUM(i.quantity_on_hand) AS qty,
       p.unit_cost,
       SUM(i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost) AS book_value,
       ROUND(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost)*0.40,2) AS liquidation_revenue_at_40pct
FROM inventory i
JOIN products p ON i.sku=p.sku
LEFT JOIN last_out lo ON i.sku=lo.sku
WHERE i.quantity_on_hand>0
  AND (lo.last_movement IS NULL OR DATEDIFF(DAY,lo.last_movement,GETDATE())>180)
GROUP BY p.sku, p.product_name, p.category, p.unit_cost
ORDER BY book_value DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: What revenue could we generate if we liquidated all dead stock at 40% of book value?
output:
SELECT SUM(i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost) AS total_book_value,
       ROUND(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost)*0.40,2) AS liquidation_revenue_40pct,
       ROUND(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost)*0.60,2) AS writeoff_loss_60pct
FROM inventory i
JOIN products p ON i.sku=p.sku
WHERE i.quantity_on_hand>0
  AND p.sku NOT IN (
      SELECT DISTINCT it.sku FROM inventory_transactions it
      WHERE it.transaction_type='OUTBOUND'
        AND it.transaction_date>=DATEADD(MONTH,-6,GETDATE())
  );

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Slow stock liquidation value — potential recovery from selling stagnant inventory at discount
output:
SELECT p.category,
       COUNT(DISTINCT p.sku) AS stagnant_skus,
       SUM(i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost) AS book_value,
       ROUND(SUM(i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost)*0.50,2) AS recovery_at_50pct_discount
FROM inventory i
JOIN products p ON i.sku=p.sku
LEFT JOIN inventory_transactions it ON i.sku=it.sku
  AND it.transaction_type='OUTBOUND'
  AND it.transaction_date>=DATEADD(DAY,-180,GETDATE())
WHERE i.quantity_on_hand>0
  AND it.transaction_id IS NULL
GROUP BY p.category
ORDER BY recovery_at_50pct_discount DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Amortize contract value over its duration — monthly cost rate for each active agreement
output:
SELECT c.contract_number, s.supplier_name,
       c.start_date, c.end_date, c.contract_value,
       DATEDIFF(MONTH,c.start_date,c.end_date) AS contract_months,
       ROUND(c.contract_value/NULLIF(DATEDIFF(MONTH,c.start_date,c.end_date),0),2) AS monthly_amortised_cost
FROM contracts c
JOIN suppliers s ON c.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE c.status='ACTIVE'
ORDER BY monthly_amortised_cost DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: What is the monthly amortised cost for each of our active contracts?
output:
SELECT c.contract_number, s.supplier_name,
       c.contract_value,
       DATEDIFF(MONTH,c.start_date,c.end_date) AS duration_months,
       ROUND(c.contract_value/NULLIF(DATEDIFF(MONTH,c.start_date,c.end_date),0),0) AS monthly_cost
FROM contracts c
JOIN suppliers s ON c.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE c.status='ACTIVE' AND DATEDIFF(MONTH,c.start_date,c.end_date)>0
ORDER BY monthly_cost DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: How should we amortize each contract across its remaining months to plan the budget?
output:
SELECT c.contract_number, s.supplier_name,
       c.contract_value,
       DATEDIFF(MONTH,GETDATE(),c.end_date) AS months_remaining,
       ROUND(c.contract_value/NULLIF(DATEDIFF(MONTH,c.start_date,c.end_date),0),2) AS total_monthly_rate,
       ROUND(c.contract_value/NULLIF(DATEDIFF(MONTH,c.start_date,c.end_date),0)*DATEDIFF(MONTH,GETDATE(),c.end_date),2) AS remaining_commitment
FROM contracts c
JOIN suppliers s ON c.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE c.status='ACTIVE'
  AND DATEDIFF(MONTH,GETDATE(),c.end_date)>0
ORDER BY remaining_commitment DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Capitalise high-value items — machinery or equipment purchases above 100K threshold
output:
SELECT po.po_number, s.supplier_name, poi.sku, p.product_name,
       poi.ordered_quantity, poi.unit_price,
       poi.ordered_quantity*poi.unit_price AS line_value
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
WHERE poi.ordered_quantity*poi.unit_price >= 100000
  AND p.product_type IN ('EQUIPMENT','MACHINERY','CAPITAL')
  AND po.status!='CANCELLED'
  AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
ORDER BY line_value DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Which procurement lines above 100K should be capitalised rather than expensed?
output:
SELECT po.po_number, s.supplier_name, p.product_type,
       SUM(poi.ordered_quantity*poi.unit_price) AS line_value
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED'
  AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY po.po_number, s.supplier_name, p.product_type
HAVING SUM(poi.ordered_quantity*poi.unit_price)>=100000
ORDER BY line_value DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Total capitalised procurement spend on equipment and machinery this year
output:
SELECT p.product_type,
       COUNT(DISTINCT po.po_id) AS pos,
       SUM(poi.line_total) AS capitalised_spend
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
WHERE p.product_type IN ('EQUIPMENT','MACHINERY','CAPITAL')
  AND po.status!='CANCELLED'
  AND YEAR(po.order_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())
GROUP BY p.product_type
ORDER BY capitalised_spend DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Provisioning gap — safety stock not configured for active finished goods
output:
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name, p.category,
       SUM(i.quantity_on_hand) AS current_stock
FROM products p
JOIN inventory i ON p.sku=i.sku
WHERE p.product_type='FINISHED_GOODS'
  AND p.status='ACTIVE'
  AND ISNULL(i.safety_stock,0)=0
GROUP BY p.sku, p.product_name, p.category
ORDER BY SUM(i.quantity_on_hand) DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Which active products have no safety stock provisioned in the inventory system?
output:
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name, p.category,
       w.warehouse_name,
       i.quantity_on_hand,
       ISNULL(i.safety_stock,0) AS safety_stock
FROM inventory i
JOIN products p ON i.sku=p.sku
JOIN warehouses w ON i.warehouse_id=w.warehouse_id
WHERE p.status='ACTIVE'
  AND ISNULL(i.safety_stock,0)=0
ORDER BY p.category, p.sku;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Is there any finished goods inventory with no safety stock provisioning — what is the exposure?
output:
SELECT p.category,
       COUNT(DISTINCT p.sku) AS unprovisioned_skus,
       SUM(i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost) AS unprotected_value
FROM inventory i
JOIN products p ON i.sku=p.sku
WHERE p.product_type='FINISHED_GOODS'
  AND ISNULL(i.safety_stock,0)=0
  AND i.quantity_on_hand>0
GROUP BY p.category
ORDER BY unprotected_value DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Cadence analysis — how regularly does each supplier ship to us on a monthly basis?
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name,
       COUNT(DISTINCT FORMAT(sh.ship_date,'yyyy-MM')) AS months_with_deliveries,
       COUNT(sh.shipment_id) AS total_shipments,
       ROUND(1.0*COUNT(sh.shipment_id)/NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT FORMAT(sh.ship_date,'yyyy-MM')),0),1) AS shipments_per_active_month
FROM shipments sh
JOIN purchase_orders po ON sh.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE sh.status='DELIVERED'
  AND sh.actual_delivery_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.supplier_name
ORDER BY shipments_per_active_month DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: What is the delivery cadence for our top vendors — how many shipments per month on average?
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name,
       COUNT(sh.shipment_id) AS total_shipments,
       ROUND(1.0*COUNT(sh.shipment_id)/12,1) AS avg_monthly_cadence
FROM shipments sh
JOIN purchase_orders po ON sh.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE sh.status='DELIVERED'
  AND sh.actual_delivery_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.supplier_name
ORDER BY avg_monthly_cadence DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Supplier delivery cadence irregularity — vendors with high variance in monthly shipment frequency
output:
WITH monthly_count AS (
    SELECT po.supplier_id,
           FORMAT(sh.actual_delivery_date,'yyyy-MM') AS month,
           COUNT(*) AS shipments
    FROM shipments sh
    JOIN purchase_orders po ON sh.po_id=po.po_id
    WHERE sh.status='DELIVERED' AND sh.actual_delivery_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
    GROUP BY po.supplier_id, FORMAT(sh.actual_delivery_date,'yyyy-MM')
)
SELECT s.supplier_name,
       AVG(mc.shipments) AS avg_monthly_shipments,
       ROUND(STDEV(mc.shipments),2) AS cadence_stdev,
       ROUND(STDEV(mc.shipments)/NULLIF(AVG(mc.shipments),0),3) AS cadence_cv
FROM monthly_count mc
JOIN suppliers s ON mc.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
GROUP BY s.supplier_name
HAVING COUNT(*)>=3
ORDER BY cadence_cv DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Demand volatility index — rank SKUs by coefficient of variation in monthly sales
output:
WITH monthly AS (
    SELECT soi.sku, FORMAT(so.order_date,'yyyy-MM') AS month,
           SUM(soi.ordered_quantity) AS qty
    FROM sales_order_items soi JOIN sales_orders so ON soi.so_id=so.so_id
    WHERE so.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE()) AND so.status!='CANCELLED'
    GROUP BY soi.sku, FORMAT(so.order_date,'yyyy-MM')
)
SELECT m.sku, p.product_name,
       ROUND(AVG(m.qty),1) AS avg_demand,
       ROUND(STDEV(m.qty),2) AS demand_stdev,
       ROUND(STDEV(m.qty)/NULLIF(AVG(m.qty),0),3) AS volatility_index,
       CASE WHEN STDEV(m.qty)/NULLIF(AVG(m.qty),0)<0.15 THEN 'LOW'
            WHEN STDEV(m.qty)/NULLIF(AVG(m.qty),0)<0.35 THEN 'MEDIUM'
            ELSE 'HIGH' END AS volatility_class
FROM monthly m
JOIN products p ON m.sku=p.sku
GROUP BY m.sku, p.product_name
HAVING COUNT(*)>=6
ORDER BY volatility_index DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: What is the demand volatility for each SKU — which products have the most unpredictable sales?
output:
WITH monthly_sales AS (
    SELECT soi.sku, FORMAT(so.order_date,'yyyy-MM') AS month, SUM(soi.ordered_quantity) AS qty
    FROM sales_order_items soi JOIN sales_orders so ON soi.so_id=so.so_id
    WHERE so.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE()) AND so.status!='CANCELLED'
    GROUP BY soi.sku, FORMAT(so.order_date,'yyyy-MM')
)
SELECT ms.sku, p.product_name,
       ROUND(STDEV(ms.qty)/NULLIF(AVG(ms.qty),0),3) AS cv
FROM monthly_sales ms
JOIN products p ON ms.sku=p.sku
GROUP BY ms.sku, p.product_name
HAVING COUNT(*)>=3
ORDER BY cv DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Is demand volatility correlated with stockout frequency across our product range?
output:
WITH volatility AS (
    SELECT soi.sku,
           ROUND(STDEV(monthly_qty.qty)/NULLIF(AVG(monthly_qty.qty),0),3) AS cv
    FROM sales_order_items soi
    JOIN sales_orders so ON soi.so_id=so.so_id
    JOIN (
        SELECT soi2.sku, FORMAT(so2.order_date,'yyyy-MM') AS ym, SUM(soi2.ordered_quantity) AS qty
        FROM sales_order_items soi2 JOIN sales_orders so2 ON soi2.so_id=so2.so_id
        WHERE so2.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
        GROUP BY soi2.sku, FORMAT(so2.order_date,'yyyy-MM')
    ) monthly_qty ON soi.sku=monthly_qty.sku
    GROUP BY soi.sku
),
stockouts AS (
    SELECT sku, COUNT(*) AS stockout_count
    FROM stockout_events WHERE start_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
    GROUP BY sku
)
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name,
       v.cv AS demand_cv,
       COALESCE(s.stockout_count,0) AS stockout_count
FROM volatility v
JOIN products p ON v.sku=p.sku
LEFT JOIN stockouts s ON v.sku=s.sku
ORDER BY v.cv DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Granularity drill-down — purchase order line item detail for a specific vendor this month
output:
SELECT po.po_number, poi.sku, p.product_name,
       poi.ordered_quantity, poi.unit_price, poi.line_total,
       po.order_date
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE s.supplier_name='FastBolt Industries'
  AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(DAY,1-DAY(GETDATE()),CAST(GETDATE() AS DATE))
  AND po.status!='CANCELLED'
ORDER BY po.order_date DESC, poi.line_total DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: PO line item granularity — every SKU, quantity and price on all orders placed this month
output:
SELECT po.po_number, s.supplier_name, poi.sku, p.product_name,
       poi.ordered_quantity, poi.unit_price, poi.line_total
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED'
  AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(DAY,1-DAY(GETDATE()),CAST(GETDATE() AS DATE))
ORDER BY po.order_date DESC, s.supplier_name, poi.line_total DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: What level of granularity can I see for this month's purchase orders — give me full line-item detail?
output:
SELECT po.po_number, po.order_date, s.supplier_name,
       poi.sku, p.product_name, p.category,
       poi.ordered_quantity, poi.unit_price,
       poi.ordered_quantity*poi.unit_price AS line_value,
       po.status
FROM purchase_orders po
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON po.po_id=poi.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
WHERE po.order_date>=DATEADD(DAY,1-DAY(GETDATE()),CAST(GETDATE() AS DATE))
ORDER BY po.order_date DESC, poi.line_value DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Periodicity of contract renewals — how many contracts come up for renewal each month?
output:
SELECT FORMAT(c.end_date,'yyyy-MM') AS renewal_month,
       COUNT(*) AS contracts_expiring,
       SUM(c.contract_value) AS value_at_renewal
FROM contracts c
JOIN suppliers s ON c.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE c.status='ACTIVE'
  AND c.end_date BETWEEN GETDATE() AND DATEADD(MONTH,12,GETDATE())
GROUP BY FORMAT(c.end_date,'yyyy-MM')
ORDER BY renewal_month;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: What is the renewal periodicity of our supplier contracts across the next 12 months?
output:
SELECT FORMAT(c.end_date,'yyyy-MM') AS month,
       COUNT(*) AS renewals_due,
       SUM(c.contract_value) AS total_contract_value
FROM contracts c
WHERE c.status='ACTIVE'
  AND c.end_date BETWEEN GETDATE() AND DATEADD(MONTH,12,GETDATE())
GROUP BY FORMAT(c.end_date,'yyyy-MM')
ORDER BY month;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Does our contract renewal periodicity cluster around certain months — peak renewal periods?
output:
SELECT DATENAME(MONTH,c.end_date) AS renewal_month_name,
       DATEPART(MONTH,c.end_date) AS month_num,
       COUNT(*) AS contracts_due,
       SUM(c.contract_value) AS value_due
FROM contracts c
WHERE c.status='ACTIVE'
  AND c.end_date BETWEEN GETDATE() AND DATEADD(YEAR,2,GETDATE())
GROUP BY DATENAME(MONTH,c.end_date), DATEPART(MONTH,c.end_date)
ORDER BY month_num;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Procurement consolidation opportunity — vendors supplying same SKU from multiple separate contracts
output:
SELECT poi.sku, p.product_name,
       COUNT(DISTINCT po.supplier_id) AS active_vendors,
       COUNT(DISTINCT po.po_id) AS total_pos,
       SUM(poi.line_total) AS total_spend
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED'
  AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY poi.sku, p.product_name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT po.supplier_id)>1
ORDER BY total_spend DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Which SKUs are being sourced from multiple vendors simultaneously — consolidation opportunity?
output:
SELECT poi.sku, p.product_name,
       COUNT(DISTINCT po.supplier_id) AS vendor_count,
       SUM(poi.line_total) AS annual_spend,
       STRING_AGG(DISTINCT s.supplier_name,', ') WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY s.supplier_name) AS vendors
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY poi.sku, p.product_name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT po.supplier_id)>1
ORDER BY vendor_count DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Quantify the savings potential from single-sourcing multi-vendor SKUs at the lowest available rate
output:
WITH multi_vendor AS (
    SELECT poi.sku, MIN(poi.unit_price) AS lowest_price
    FROM purchase_order_items poi
    JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
    WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
    GROUP BY poi.sku
    HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT po.supplier_id)>1
)
SELECT p.product_name,
       SUM(poi.ordered_quantity*poi.unit_price) AS actual_spend,
       SUM(poi.ordered_quantity*mv.lowest_price) AS optimal_spend,
       SUM(poi.ordered_quantity*(poi.unit_price-mv.lowest_price)) AS consolidation_saving
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN multi_vendor mv ON poi.sku=mv.sku
JOIN products p ON poi.sku=p.sku
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY p.product_name
ORDER BY consolidation_saving DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Quality control acceptance rate — percentage of goods receipts that passed first-time inspection
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name,
       COUNT(*) AS inspections,
       SUM(CASE WHEN qc.result='PASS' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS first_time_pass,
       ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN qc.result='PASS' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/NULLIF(COUNT(*),0),1) AS first_pass_rate
FROM quality_checks qc
JOIN goods_receipt_items gri ON qc.grn_item_id=gri.grn_item_id
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON gri.po_item_id=poi.po_item_id
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE qc.inspection_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.supplier_name
ORDER BY first_pass_rate;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: What is the first-time pass rate for quality inspections across all active vendors?
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name,
       COUNT(*) AS total_inspections,
       ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN qc.result='PASS' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/NULLIF(COUNT(*),0),1) AS pass_rate_pct
FROM quality_checks qc
JOIN goods_receipt_items gri ON qc.grn_item_id=gri.grn_item_id
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON gri.po_item_id=poi.po_item_id
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE qc.inspection_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE()) AND s.status='ACTIVE'
GROUP BY s.supplier_name
HAVING COUNT(*)>=5
ORDER BY pass_rate_pct;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Has the quality acceptance rate for our top vendor improved compared to last year?
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name,
       ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN qc.result='PASS' AND YEAR(qc.inspection_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())-1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
             /NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN YEAR(qc.inspection_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())-1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END),0),1) AS pass_rate_prev_year,
       ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN qc.result='PASS' AND YEAR(qc.inspection_date)=YEAR(GETDATE()) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
             /NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN YEAR(qc.inspection_date)=YEAR(GETDATE()) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END),0),1) AS pass_rate_this_year
FROM quality_checks qc
JOIN goods_receipt_items gri ON qc.grn_item_id=gri.grn_item_id
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON gri.po_item_id=poi.po_item_id
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
GROUP BY s.supplier_name
HAVING COUNT(*)>=5
ORDER BY pass_rate_this_year-pass_rate_prev_year ASC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Financial exposure from supplier payment terms — total outstanding by NET30 NET60 NET90 buckets
output:
SELECT s.payment_terms,
       COUNT(inv.invoice_id) AS invoices,
       SUM(inv.total_amount-COALESCE(inv.paid_amount,0)) AS outstanding_balance
FROM invoices inv
JOIN suppliers s ON inv.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE inv.status IN ('UNPAID','PARTIALLY_PAID')
GROUP BY s.payment_terms
ORDER BY outstanding_balance DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: How is our outstanding payables exposure distributed across payment term buckets?
output:
SELECT s.payment_terms,
       SUM(inv.total_amount-COALESCE(inv.paid_amount,0)) AS outstanding,
       ROUND(100.0*SUM(inv.total_amount-COALESCE(inv.paid_amount,0))
             /SUM(SUM(inv.total_amount-COALESCE(inv.paid_amount,0))) OVER (),1) AS pct
FROM invoices inv
JOIN suppliers s ON inv.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE inv.status IN ('UNPAID','PARTIALLY_PAID')
GROUP BY s.payment_terms
ORDER BY outstanding DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Is the bulk of our payables exposure sitting with vendors on extended NET60 or NET90 terms?
output:
SELECT CASE WHEN s.payment_terms IN ('NET30') THEN 'SHORT (≤30d)'
            WHEN s.payment_terms IN ('NET60') THEN 'MEDIUM (60d)'
            WHEN s.payment_terms IN ('NET90') THEN 'EXTENDED (90d)'
            ELSE 'OTHER' END AS terms_bucket,
       SUM(inv.total_amount-COALESCE(inv.paid_amount,0)) AS exposure
FROM invoices inv
JOIN suppliers s ON inv.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE inv.status IN ('UNPAID','PARTIALLY_PAID')
GROUP BY CASE WHEN s.payment_terms IN ('NET30') THEN 'SHORT (≤30d)'
              WHEN s.payment_terms IN ('NET60') THEN 'MEDIUM (60d)'
              WHEN s.payment_terms IN ('NET90') THEN 'EXTENDED (90d)'
              ELSE 'OTHER' END
ORDER BY exposure DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Which customer segments have the highest order fulfilment cycle time this year?
output:
SELECT c.segment,
       COUNT(*) AS orders,
       ROUND(AVG(DATEDIFF(DAY,so.order_date,so.actual_delivery_date)),1) AS avg_cycle_days,
       MAX(DATEDIFF(DAY,so.order_date,so.actual_delivery_date)) AS worst_case_days
FROM customers c
JOIN sales_orders so ON c.customer_id=so.customer_id
WHERE so.status='DELIVERED'
  AND so.actual_delivery_date IS NOT NULL
  AND YEAR(so.order_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())
GROUP BY c.segment
ORDER BY avg_cycle_days DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Average order fulfilment cycle time by customer segment — who waits longest?
output:
SELECT c.segment,
       ROUND(AVG(DATEDIFF(DAY,so.order_date,so.ship_date)),1) AS avg_days_to_ship,
       COUNT(*) AS orders
FROM customers c
JOIN sales_orders so ON c.customer_id=so.customer_id
WHERE so.ship_date IS NOT NULL
  AND so.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY c.segment
ORDER BY avg_days_to_ship DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Has the order fulfilment cycle time improved for enterprise customers comparing this year to last?
output:
SELECT c.segment,
       ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN YEAR(so.order_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())-1 THEN DATEDIFF(DAY,so.order_date,so.actual_delivery_date) END),1) AS prev_year_days,
       ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN YEAR(so.order_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())   THEN DATEDIFF(DAY,so.order_date,so.actual_delivery_date) END),1) AS this_year_days
FROM customers c
JOIN sales_orders so ON c.customer_id=so.customer_id
WHERE so.status='DELIVERED' AND so.actual_delivery_date IS NOT NULL
  AND YEAR(so.order_date) IN (YEAR(GETDATE()), YEAR(GETDATE())-1)
GROUP BY c.segment
ORDER BY this_year_days;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Warehouse receiving efficiency — how does goods receipt volume vary by day of the week?
output:
SELECT DATENAME(WEEKDAY,gr.receipt_date) AS day_name,
       DATEPART(WEEKDAY,gr.receipt_date) AS day_num,
       COUNT(gr.grn_id) AS grns,
       SUM(gri.received_quantity) AS units_received,
       ROUND(AVG(gri.received_quantity*1.0),1) AS avg_units_per_grn
FROM goods_receipts gr
JOIN goods_receipt_items gri ON gr.grn_id=gri.grn_id
WHERE gr.receipt_date>=DATEADD(MONTH,-3,GETDATE())
GROUP BY DATENAME(WEEKDAY,gr.receipt_date), DATEPART(WEEKDAY,gr.receipt_date)
ORDER BY day_num;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Do we receive more goods on certain days of the week — warehouse planning insight?
output:
SELECT DATENAME(WEEKDAY,gr.receipt_date) AS weekday,
       COUNT(*) AS grns, SUM(gri.received_quantity) AS units
FROM goods_receipts gr
JOIN goods_receipt_items gri ON gr.grn_id=gri.grn_id
WHERE gr.receipt_date>=DATEADD(MONTH,-3,GETDATE())
GROUP BY DATENAME(WEEKDAY,gr.receipt_date), DATEPART(WEEKDAY,gr.receipt_date)
ORDER BY DATEPART(WEEKDAY,gr.receipt_date);

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Is there a day-of-week pattern in our warehouse inbound throughput that we should staff for?
output:
SELECT DATENAME(WEEKDAY,it.transaction_date) AS weekday,
       DATEPART(WEEKDAY,it.transaction_date) AS day_num,
       COUNT(*) AS transactions,
       SUM(it.quantity) AS units_inbound
FROM inventory_transactions it
WHERE it.transaction_type='INBOUND'
  AND it.transaction_date>=DATEADD(MONTH,-3,GETDATE())
GROUP BY DATENAME(WEEKDAY,it.transaction_date), DATEPART(WEEKDAY,it.transaction_date)
ORDER BY day_num;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Transshipping cost analysis — extra freight incurred on shipments routed via intermediate hubs
output:
SELECT r.origin, r.via_hub, r.destination, sh.carrier,
       COUNT(*) AS transshipped_shipments,
       AVG(sh.freight_cost) AS avg_freight_via_hub,
       AVG(sh.freight_cost)-AVG(direct.avg_direct) AS hub_premium
FROM shipments sh
JOIN routes r ON sh.route_id=r.route_id
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT r2.origin, r2.destination, AVG(sh2.freight_cost) AS avg_direct
    FROM shipments sh2 JOIN routes r2 ON sh2.route_id=r2.route_id
    WHERE r2.via_hub IS NULL AND sh2.status='DELIVERED'
    GROUP BY r2.origin, r2.destination
) direct ON r.origin=direct.origin AND r.destination=direct.destination
WHERE r.via_hub IS NOT NULL AND sh.status='DELIVERED'
  AND sh.actual_delivery_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY r.origin, r.via_hub, r.destination, sh.carrier
ORDER BY hub_premium DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: How much extra does transshipping via a hub cost compared to direct routing?
output:
SELECT lp.service_type, sh.carrier,
       COUNT(*) AS shipments,
       SUM(CASE WHEN r.via_hub IS NOT NULL THEN sh.freight_cost ELSE 0 END)/NULLIF(COUNT(CASE WHEN r.via_hub IS NOT NULL THEN 1 END),0) AS avg_via_hub,
       SUM(CASE WHEN r.via_hub IS NULL THEN sh.freight_cost ELSE 0 END)/NULLIF(COUNT(CASE WHEN r.via_hub IS NULL THEN 1 END),0) AS avg_direct
FROM shipments sh
JOIN routes r ON sh.route_id=r.route_id
JOIN logistics_partners lp ON sh.logistics_partner_id=lp.partner_id
WHERE sh.status='DELIVERED' AND sh.actual_delivery_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY lp.service_type, sh.carrier
ORDER BY avg_via_hub DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Are transshipping delays longer than direct routes on average?
output:
SELECT IIF(r.via_hub IS NOT NULL,'VIA HUB','DIRECT') AS routing_type,
       COUNT(*) AS shipments,
       AVG(DATEDIFF(DAY,sh.expected_delivery_date,sh.actual_delivery_date)) AS avg_delay_days,
       ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN sh.actual_delivery_date>sh.expected_delivery_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/NULLIF(COUNT(*),0),1) AS late_pct
FROM shipments sh
JOIN routes r ON sh.route_id=r.route_id
WHERE sh.status='DELIVERED' AND sh.actual_delivery_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY IIF(r.via_hub IS NOT NULL,'VIA HUB','DIRECT')
ORDER BY avg_delay_days DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Palletizing efficiency — average units per pallet and freight cost per pallet by carrier
output:
SELECT sh.carrier,
       COUNT(*) AS shipments,
       ROUND(AVG(sh.weight_kg/NULLIF(sh.pallet_count,0)),1) AS avg_kg_per_pallet,
       ROUND(AVG(sh.freight_cost/NULLIF(sh.pallet_count,0)),2) AS avg_cost_per_pallet
FROM shipments sh
WHERE sh.pallet_count IS NOT NULL AND sh.pallet_count>0
  AND sh.status='DELIVERED' AND sh.actual_delivery_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY sh.carrier
ORDER BY avg_cost_per_pallet;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Are we maximising palletizing efficiency — is each pallet carrying enough weight?
output:
SELECT sh.carrier, lp.service_type,
       COUNT(*) AS shipments,
       ROUND(AVG(sh.weight_kg),1) AS avg_total_weight,
       ROUND(AVG(sh.pallet_count),1) AS avg_pallets,
       ROUND(AVG(sh.weight_kg/NULLIF(sh.pallet_count,0)),1) AS avg_weight_per_pallet,
       IIF(AVG(sh.weight_kg/NULLIF(sh.pallet_count,0))<500,'UNDERLOADED','OK') AS loading_status
FROM shipments sh
JOIN logistics_partners lp ON sh.logistics_partner_id=lp.partner_id
WHERE sh.pallet_count>0 AND sh.status='DELIVERED'
  AND sh.actual_delivery_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY sh.carrier, lp.service_type
ORDER BY avg_weight_per_pallet;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Unitizing variance — shipments where actual pallet count exceeded the expected plan
output:
SELECT sh.tracking_number, sh.carrier,
       sh.pallet_count AS actual_pallets,
       sh.planned_pallet_count AS planned_pallets,
       sh.pallet_count-sh.planned_pallet_count AS pallet_overrun,
       (sh.pallet_count-sh.planned_pallet_count)*50 AS estimated_extra_cost
FROM shipments sh
WHERE sh.pallet_count > sh.planned_pallet_count
  AND sh.status='DELIVERED'
  AND sh.actual_delivery_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
ORDER BY pallet_overrun DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Escalated supplier issues — vendors with 3 or more unresolved corrective actions open right now
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name, s.category,
       COUNT(ca.ca_id) AS open_cas,
       MIN(ca.raised_date) AS oldest_open_date,
       DATEDIFF(DAY,MIN(ca.raised_date),GETDATE()) AS oldest_days_open,
       SUM(CASE WHEN DATEDIFF(DAY,ca.raised_date,GETDATE())>ca.sla_days THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS sla_breaches
FROM corrective_actions ca
JOIN suppliers s ON ca.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE ca.resolved_date IS NULL
GROUP BY s.supplier_name, s.category
HAVING COUNT(ca.ca_id)>=3
ORDER BY open_cas DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Which quality issues have been escalated beyond the SLA resolution deadline?
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name, ca.sku, p.product_name,
       ca.raised_date, ca.sla_days,
       DATEDIFF(DAY,ca.raised_date,GETDATE()) AS days_open,
       DATEDIFF(DAY,ca.raised_date,GETDATE())-ca.sla_days AS days_past_sla
FROM corrective_actions ca
JOIN suppliers s ON ca.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
JOIN products p ON ca.sku=p.sku
WHERE ca.resolved_date IS NULL
  AND DATEDIFF(DAY,ca.raised_date,GETDATE()) > ca.sla_days
ORDER BY days_past_sla DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Are there any quality corrective actions that were escalated to management due to repeated failure?
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name,
       COUNT(DISTINCT ca.sku) AS skus_with_escalated_cas,
       COUNT(ca.ca_id) AS total_escalated
FROM corrective_actions ca
JOIN suppliers s ON ca.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE ca.escalated_to_management=1
  AND ca.raised_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.supplier_name
ORDER BY total_escalated DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Decommission review — products discontinued over 6 months ago still holding inventory value
output:
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name, p.category,
       p.discontinued_date,
       DATEDIFF(DAY,p.discontinued_date,GETDATE()) AS days_since_decommission,
       SUM(i.quantity_on_hand) AS residual_qty,
       SUM(i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost) AS residual_value
FROM products p
JOIN inventory i ON p.sku=i.sku
WHERE p.status='DISCONTINUED'
  AND p.discontinued_date < DATEADD(MONTH,-6,GETDATE())
  AND i.quantity_on_hand>0
GROUP BY p.sku, p.product_name, p.category, p.unit_cost, p.discontinued_date
ORDER BY residual_value DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Harmonize supplier payment terms — identify vendors on non-standard terms for renegotiation
output:
SELECT s.payment_terms,
       COUNT(DISTINCT s.supplier_id) AS vendor_count,
       SUM(poi.line_total) AS annual_spend,
       CASE WHEN s.payment_terms NOT IN ('NET30','NET60','NET90') THEN 'NON-STANDARD' ELSE 'STANDARD' END AS terms_status
FROM suppliers s
JOIN purchase_orders po ON s.supplier_id=po.supplier_id
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON po.po_id=poi.po_id
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.payment_terms
ORDER BY CASE WHEN s.payment_terms NOT IN ('NET30','NET60','NET90') THEN 0 ELSE 1 END, annual_spend DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Standardize quality inspection frequency — suppliers below the minimum 4 inspections per year
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name, s.category,
       COUNT(DISTINCT FORMAT(qc.inspection_date,'yyyy-MM')) AS months_with_inspections,
       COUNT(qc.qc_id) AS total_inspections
FROM suppliers s
JOIN purchase_orders po ON s.supplier_id=po.supplier_id
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON po.po_id=poi.po_id
JOIN goods_receipt_items gri ON poi.po_item_id=gri.po_item_id
JOIN quality_checks qc ON gri.grn_item_id=qc.grn_item_id
WHERE qc.inspection_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.supplier_name, s.category
HAVING COUNT(qc.qc_id)<4
ORDER BY total_inspections;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Digitalize invoice processing — what percentage of invoices are submitted electronically?
output:
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_invoices,
    SUM(CASE WHEN inv.invoice_source='ELECTRONIC' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS electronic,
    SUM(CASE WHEN inv.invoice_source='PAPER' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS paper,
    ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN inv.invoice_source='ELECTRONIC' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/NULLIF(COUNT(*),0),1) AS digitalized_pct
FROM invoices inv
WHERE inv.invoice_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE());

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Optimize reorder quantity — compare current order sizes to EOQ recommendations
output:
SELECT eoq.sku, p.product_name,
       eoq.economic_order_qty AS optimal_qty,
       ROUND(AVG(poi.ordered_quantity),0) AS actual_avg_qty,
       ROUND(AVG(poi.ordered_quantity)-eoq.economic_order_qty,0) AS deviation,
       ROUND(100.0*(AVG(poi.ordered_quantity)-eoq.economic_order_qty)/NULLIF(eoq.economic_order_qty,0),1) AS deviation_pct
FROM eoq_parameters eoq
JOIN products p ON eoq.sku=p.sku
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON eoq.sku=poi.sku
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND po.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY eoq.sku, p.product_name, eoq.economic_order_qty
ORDER BY ABS(ROUND(AVG(poi.ordered_quantity)-eoq.economic_order_qty,0)) DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Normalize supplier defect rates to compare across different volume levels
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name,
       SUM(gri.received_quantity) AS total_received,
       SUM(qc.defective_quantity) AS total_defective,
       ROUND(100.0*SUM(qc.defective_quantity)/NULLIF(SUM(gri.received_quantity),0),3) AS normalized_defect_rate,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY 100.0*SUM(qc.defective_quantity)/NULLIF(SUM(gri.received_quantity),0) ASC) AS quality_rank
FROM quality_checks qc
JOIN goods_receipt_items gri ON qc.grn_item_id=gri.grn_item_id
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON gri.po_item_id=poi.po_item_id
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE qc.inspection_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.supplier_name
HAVING SUM(gri.received_quantity)>=100
ORDER BY normalized_defect_rate;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Annualized procurement spend projection — current year run-rate extrapolated to 12 months
output:
SELECT s.category,
       SUM(poi.line_total) AS ytd_spend,
       DATEPART(DAYOFYEAR,GETDATE()) AS days_elapsed,
       ROUND(SUM(poi.line_total)/DATEPART(DAYOFYEAR,GETDATE())*365,0) AS annualized_projection
FROM purchase_orders po
JOIN purchase_order_items poi ON po.po_id=poi.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED'
  AND YEAR(po.order_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.category
ORDER BY annualized_projection DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: What is the annualized run-rate for each supplier based on year-to-date spend?
output:
SELECT s.supplier_name,
       SUM(poi.line_total) AS ytd_spend,
       ROUND(SUM(poi.line_total)/NULLIF(DATEPART(DAYOFYEAR,GETDATE()),0)*365,0) AS annualized_spend
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
JOIN suppliers s ON po.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED'
  AND YEAR(po.order_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())
GROUP BY s.supplier_name
ORDER BY annualized_spend DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Will we exceed last year's annualized procurement budget based on current spending pace?
output:
SELECT
    ROUND(SUM(poi.line_total)/NULLIF(DATEPART(DAYOFYEAR,GETDATE()),0)*365,0) AS annualized_current,
    (
        SELECT SUM(poi2.line_total) FROM purchase_order_items poi2
        JOIN purchase_orders po2 ON poi2.po_id=po2.po_id
        WHERE po2.status!='CANCELLED' AND YEAR(po2.order_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())-1
    ) AS last_year_actual,
    ROUND(SUM(poi.line_total)/NULLIF(DATEPART(DAYOFYEAR,GETDATE()),0)*365,0)
    - (
        SELECT SUM(poi2.line_total) FROM purchase_order_items poi2
        JOIN purchase_orders po2 ON poi2.po_id=po2.po_id
        WHERE po2.status!='CANCELLED' AND YEAR(po2.order_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())-1
    ) AS projected_variance
FROM purchase_order_items poi
JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
WHERE po.status!='CANCELLED' AND YEAR(po.order_date)=YEAR(GETDATE());

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Indexed price tracking — how have unit prices moved versus the baseline 12 months ago?
output:
WITH base AS (
    SELECT poi.sku, AVG(poi.unit_price) AS base_price
    FROM purchase_order_items poi
    JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
    WHERE po.order_date BETWEEN DATEADD(YEAR,-1,DATEADD(MONTH,-1,GETDATE())) AND DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
      AND po.status!='CANCELLED'
    GROUP BY poi.sku
),
current_p AS (
    SELECT poi.sku, AVG(poi.unit_price) AS curr_price
    FROM purchase_order_items poi
    JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
    WHERE po.order_date>=DATEADD(MONTH,-1,GETDATE()) AND po.status!='CANCELLED'
    GROUP BY poi.sku
)
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name,
       ROUND(b.base_price,2) AS base_price_12m_ago,
       ROUND(c.curr_price,2) AS current_price,
       ROUND(100.0*c.curr_price/NULLIF(b.base_price,0),1) AS price_index,
       ROUND(100.0*(c.curr_price-b.base_price)/NULLIF(b.base_price,0),1) AS pct_change
FROM base b
JOIN current_p c ON b.sku=c.sku
JOIN products p ON b.sku=p.sku
ORDER BY pct_change DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Are purchase prices indexed higher than 12 months ago for critical raw materials?
output:
WITH yr_ago AS (
    SELECT poi.sku, AVG(poi.unit_price) AS old_price
    FROM purchase_order_items poi
    JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
    WHERE po.order_date BETWEEN DATEADD(YEAR,-1,DATEADD(MONTH,-3,GETDATE())) AND DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
      AND po.status!='CANCELLED'
    GROUP BY poi.sku
),
now_p AS (
    SELECT poi.sku, AVG(poi.unit_price) AS new_price
    FROM purchase_order_items poi
    JOIN purchase_orders po ON poi.po_id=po.po_id
    WHERE po.order_date>=DATEADD(MONTH,-3,GETDATE()) AND po.status!='CANCELLED'
    GROUP BY poi.sku
)
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name,
       ROUND(ya.old_price,2) AS price_yr_ago,
       ROUND(np.new_price,2) AS current_price,
       ROUND(100.0*(np.new_price-ya.old_price)/NULLIF(ya.old_price,0),1) AS indexed_change_pct
FROM yr_ago ya
JOIN now_p np ON ya.sku=np.sku
JOIN products p ON ya.sku=p.sku
WHERE p.product_type='RAW_MATERIAL'
ORDER BY indexed_change_pct DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Apportioned warehouse costs — total holding expense split across product categories by occupancy
output:
WITH occupancy AS (
    SELECT p.category, SUM(i.quantity_on_hand*p.unit_cost) AS category_value
    FROM inventory i JOIN products p ON i.sku=p.sku
    GROUP BY p.category
),
total_occ AS (
    SELECT SUM(category_value) AS grand_total FROM occupancy
)
SELECT o.category,
       o.category_value,
       ROUND(o.category_value/NULLIF(t.grand_total,0)*100,2) AS pct_of_total,
       ROUND(o.category_value/NULLIF(t.grand_total,0)*500000,0) AS apportioned_holding_cost
FROM occupancy o
CROSS JOIN total_occ t
ORDER BY apportioned_holding_cost DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Haulier performance comparison — on-time delivery rate and damage rate per logistics provider
output:
SELECT lp.partner_name AS haulier, lp.service_type,
       COUNT(*) AS shipments,
       ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN sh.actual_delivery_date<=sh.expected_delivery_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/NULLIF(COUNT(*),0),1) AS otd_pct,
       ROUND(100.0*SUM(sh.damage_reported)/NULLIF(COUNT(*),0),2) AS damage_rate_pct
FROM logistics_partners lp
JOIN shipments sh ON lp.partner_id=sh.logistics_partner_id
WHERE sh.status='DELIVERED' AND sh.actual_delivery_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE())
GROUP BY lp.partner_name, lp.service_type
ORDER BY otd_pct DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Which haulier has the best on-time delivery record for sea freight this year?
output:
SELECT lp.partner_name AS haulier,
       COUNT(*) AS sea_shipments,
       ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN sh.actual_delivery_date<=sh.expected_delivery_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/NULLIF(COUNT(*),0),1) AS otd_pct
FROM logistics_partners lp
JOIN shipments sh ON lp.partner_id=sh.logistics_partner_id
WHERE lp.service_type='SEA' AND sh.status='DELIVERED'
  AND YEAR(sh.actual_delivery_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())
GROUP BY lp.partner_name
ORDER BY otd_pct DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Has any haulier's performance deteriorated significantly in the last quarter versus the prior quarter?
output:
WITH qotd AS (
    SELECT sh.logistics_partner_id,
           DATEPART(QUARTER,sh.actual_delivery_date) AS qtr,
           YEAR(sh.actual_delivery_date) AS yr,
           ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN sh.actual_delivery_date<=sh.expected_delivery_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/NULLIF(COUNT(*),0),1) AS otd
    FROM shipments sh
    WHERE sh.status='DELIVERED' AND sh.actual_delivery_date>=DATEADD(QUARTER,-2,GETDATE())
    GROUP BY sh.logistics_partner_id, DATEPART(QUARTER,sh.actual_delivery_date), YEAR(sh.actual_delivery_date)
)
SELECT lp.partner_name AS haulier,
       MAX(CASE WHEN qtr=DATEPART(QUARTER,GETDATE())-1 THEN otd END) AS prev_qtr_otd,
       MAX(CASE WHEN qtr=DATEPART(QUARTER,GETDATE()) THEN otd END) AS curr_qtr_otd,
       MAX(CASE WHEN qtr=DATEPART(QUARTER,GETDATE()) THEN otd END)
       - MAX(CASE WHEN qtr=DATEPART(QUARTER,GETDATE())-1 THEN otd END) AS qtr_change
FROM qotd q
JOIN logistics_partners lp ON q.logistics_partner_id=lp.partner_id
GROUP BY lp.partner_name
HAVING MAX(CASE WHEN qtr=DATEPART(QUARTER,GETDATE()) THEN otd END)
       - MAX(CASE WHEN qtr=DATEPART(QUARTER,GETDATE())-1 THEN otd END) < -10
ORDER BY qtr_change;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: KPI milestone check — suppliers who hit their delivery performance target every quarter this year
output:
WITH quarterly AS (
    SELECT po.supplier_id, DATEPART(QUARTER,sh.actual_delivery_date) AS qtr,
           ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN sh.actual_delivery_date<=sh.expected_delivery_date THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/NULLIF(COUNT(*),0),1) AS otd
    FROM purchase_orders po
    JOIN shipments sh ON po.po_id=sh.po_id
    WHERE sh.status='DELIVERED' AND YEAR(sh.actual_delivery_date)=YEAR(GETDATE())
    GROUP BY po.supplier_id, DATEPART(QUARTER,sh.actual_delivery_date)
)
SELECT s.supplier_name,
       COUNT(DISTINCT q.qtr) AS quarters_meeting_kpi,
       MIN(q.otd) AS lowest_quarterly_otd
FROM quarterly q
JOIN suppliers s ON q.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
WHERE q.otd>=90
GROUP BY s.supplier_name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT q.qtr)=DATEPART(QUARTER,GETDATE())
ORDER BY lowest_quarterly_otd DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: SLA checkpoint — which supplier contracts have a delivery SLA clause and are we meeting it?
output:
SELECT c.contract_number, s.supplier_name,
       c.delivery_sla_days,
       ROUND(AVG(DATEDIFF(DAY,po.order_date,sh.actual_delivery_date)),1) AS avg_actual_lead_days,
       IIF(AVG(DATEDIFF(DAY,po.order_date,sh.actual_delivery_date))<=c.delivery_sla_days,'MEETING SLA','BREACHING SLA') AS sla_status
FROM contracts c
JOIN suppliers s ON c.supplier_id=s.supplier_id
JOIN purchase_orders po ON po.supplier_id=c.supplier_id
  AND po.order_date BETWEEN c.start_date AND c.end_date
JOIN shipments sh ON po.po_id=sh.po_id
WHERE c.status='ACTIVE' AND sh.status='DELIVERED'
  AND c.delivery_sla_days IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY c.contract_number, s.supplier_name, c.delivery_sla_days
ORDER BY sla_status DESC;

instruction: Convert the following natural language question to a SQL query for a Supply Chain Management database.
input: Data-driven reorder points — calculate statistically optimal reorder triggers using actual demand history
output:
WITH monthly AS (
    SELECT soi.sku, FORMAT(so.order_date,'yyyy-MM') AS ym, SUM(soi.ordered_quantity) AS qty
    FROM sales_order_items soi JOIN sales_orders so ON soi.so_id=so.so_id
    WHERE so.order_date>=DATEADD(YEAR,-1,GETDATE()) AND so.status!='CANCELLED'
    GROUP BY soi.sku, FORMAT(so.order_date,'yyyy-MM')
)
SELECT p.sku, p.product_name, p.lead_time_days,
